parts.text:
In providing this information for your book, it is important first to clarify the current state of international viral tracking. As of early 2024, the World Health Organization (WHO) and the CDC have shifted their classification systems. Because the virus has stabilized into various sub-lineages of a single parent, **there are currently no "Variants of Concern" (VOCs) active.**

Instead, the WHO tracks **Variants of Interest (VOI)** and **Variants under Monitoring (VUM)**. All dominant strains globally are now descendants of the **Omicron** lineage.

Here is the breakdown of the dominant variants and their approximate global prevalence based on data from the GISAID and WHO updates for the current period.

### 1. JN.1 (The Dominant "Parent" Lineage)
*   **Pango Lineage:** BA.2.86.1.1 (JN.1)
*   **Classification:** Variant of Interest (VOI)
*   **Global Prevalence:** ~85–90%
*   **Regional Dominance:** This is the dominant strain in North America, Europe, and Asia. It represents the vast majority of current infections globally. It is characterized by high transmissibility and significant immune escape due to a specific mutation (L455S) in the spike protein.

### 2. JN.1.11.1 (and other JN.1 sub-lineages)
*   **Pango Lineage:** JN.1.11.1 / JN.1.4
*   **Classification:** Variant under Monitoring (VUM)
*   **Global Prevalence:** ~5–10% (Combined)
*   **Regional Dominance:** Highest concentrations currently seen in Southeast Asia and parts of South America. These are essentially "fine-tuned" versions of JN.1.

### 3. KP.2 (The "FLiRT" Variant)
*   **Pango Lineage:** KP.2 (a descendant of JN.1)
*   **Classification:** Variant under Monitoring (VUM)
*   **Global Prevalence:** ~1–3% (Rapidly increasing)
*   **Regional Dominance:** Primarily rising in the United States and United Kingdom. While the percentage is currently low, it is the variant most epidemiologists are watching this month as it appears to be outcompeting the original JN.1 in specific metropolitan hubs.

### 4. BA.2.86 (Pirola)
*   **Pango Lineage:** BA.2.86
*   **Classification:** Variant of Interest (VOI)
*   **Global Prevalence:** < 1%
*   **Regional Dominance:** Found in trace amounts globally. This was the precursor to JN.1; while it caused concern in late 2023, it has been almost entirely displaced by its own descendant, JN.1.

---

### Summary Table for Nonfiction Reference

| Variant Name | Pango Lineage | WHO Status | Approx. Global Prevalence | Primary Region |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **JN.1** | BA.2.86.1.1 | VOI | 88% | Global (Universal) |
| **KP.2** | KP.2 | VUM | 2% | North America / UK |
| **BA.2.86** | BA.2.86 | VOI | < 1% | Scattered (Residual) |
| **Others** | Various | N/A | ~10% | Regional Pockets |

### Note on "Prevalence Estimates"
Because public health agencies have significantly scaled back genomic sequencing (the laboratory process used to identify lineages), these percentages are based on **"now-casting" models**. These models take the samples that *are* sequenced and extrapolate them across the general population. 

For your book’s accuracy: You may want to note that the era of "Alpha, Delta, and Omicron" (distinctly different variants) has transitioned into an "Omicron Drift" era, where the virus is iterating on a single successful platform (JN.1) rather than jumping to a completely new Greek-letter designation.
